digest.top_line: ARS AUC for locoregionally recurrent rectal cancer: reaffirms neoadjuvant combined-modality therapy (RT ± systemic) to enable the R0 resection that drives survival; no practice change.
digest.tldr: Quiet day: a single ARS Appropriate Use Criteria update on locoregionally recurrent rectal cancer, consensus-graded (10 RCTs of 116 refs). R0 resection remains the survival driver; RT/reirradiation is rated at the topic level with no new effect sizes, so nothing shifts the reader's RT calculus.

sites.intro: Sole update today: an appropriate-use consensus rather than new randomized data, with RT/reirradiation rated at the topic level only.

studies.name: ARS Appropriate Use Criteria: Locoregionally Recurrent Rectal Cancer
studies.tldr: 116 trials (only 10 RCTs) reviewed; no major practice change, reaffirms combined-modality neoadjuvant therapy to enable the margin-negative resection that drives survival.
details: 🔍 ARS Appropriate Use Criteria: systematic review (PICOTS + PRISMA) + modified Delphi (RAND/UCLA) consensus, two voting rounds
🔍 Multispecialty panel: colorectal surgery, rad onc, med onc, surgical onc, GI, radiology

details.text: 📊 Evidence base (116 references, Jan 2013–Jul 2025) by design quality

details: 📊 R0 (margin-negative) resection is the ultimate determinant of survival and local control
💊 Preop systemic therapy, RT, or both facilitate downsizing → improve likelihood of R0 resection
🔍 Five PICO questions: surgery, preop/periop therapy, nonoperative mgmt, RT/reirradiation, systemic therapy
🔍 RT/reirradiation covered as one PICO topic; no effect sizes reported in source excerpt (topic-level appropriateness ratings)
⚠️ 76/116 references retrospective; only 10 RCTs — recommendations are consensus-graded, not driven by new randomized data
🔗 Updates the 2012 ACR recurrent rectal cancer AUC; 13 of 43 original citations retained
🔍 Workup: high-res pelvic MRI preferred for extent/resectability; FDG-PET/CT to distinguish fibrosis from active tumor
studies.significance: The RT read is scope, not magnitude: neoadjuvant RT or reirradiation is endorsed mainly as a route to margin-negative resection (the sole survival/LC determinant), not as independent therapy. It rests on 10 RCTs against 76 retrospective series, so reirradiation dose and technique stay consensus-driven, not evidence-fixed.
studies.monday_clinic: In a pt with resectable locoregionally recurrent rectal cancer, this supports neoadjuvant RT or reirradiation to improve the chance of a margin-negative resection; it does not define a reirradiation dose or extend to unresectable disease.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: **ARS Appropriate Use Criteria** systematic review using the **PICOTS + PRISMA 2020** framework and **modified Delphi (RAND/UCLA)** consensus over two voting rounds. Multispecialty committee spanning colorectal surgery, radiation, medical and surgical oncology, gastroenterology, and radiology.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: LRRC studies published **Jan 1, 2013–Jul 16, 2025**: prospective observational, phase 2/3, and retrospective series of **≥25 patients**, English-language. Of 244 identified, 80 met criteria; 23 added via citation searching.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: **116 references** used as evidence: **10** well-designed RCTs (phase 2/3), **29** moderately designed (matched cohort/phase 2), **76** retrospective, **1** meta-analysis. **R0 resection is the ultimate determinant** of survival and local control; preop systemic therapy and/or RT improve R0 likelihood.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: RT/reirradiation is **one of five PICO topics**, endorsed chiefly to downsize disease and enable a margin-negative resection rather than as independent therapy. **No reirradiation dose, fractionation, or effect sizes** are reported in the source excerpt.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: **65% of the evidence (76/116) is retrospective**, with only 10 RCTs, so ratings are **consensus-graded**. The panel states it **does not suggest a major alteration of current practice**.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents pts with locoregionally recurrent rectal cancer being evaluated for multidisciplinary curative-intent management. Does not represent de novo primary rectal cancer or pts with widely metastatic disease outside a locoregional focus.

open_questions: Role of nonoperative management in LRRC
Sequencing of systemic vs RT before resection

related_trials.brief_title: Pencil Beam Proton Therapy for Pelvic Recurrences in Rectal Cancer Patients Previously Treated With Radiotherapy
related_trials.brief_summary:
Study design:

A prospective phase II, non-randomized observational study of dose-escalated pencil beam proton therapy re-irradiation for pelvic recurrences from rectal cancer.

Re-irradiation is either a part of neo-adjuvant treatment or as a definitive treatment strategy for un-resectable recurrences.
related_trials.eligibility_brief:
Inclusion Criteria:

* • Locally recurrent rectal cancer.

  * Bioptically verified (adenocarcinoma )
  * Available dose plan from primary radiotherapy
  * Previous pelvic RT (\>30Gy EQD2)
  * Evaluated in MDT-conferences (Herlev, Aarhus)
  * Age\>18 years
  * PS 0-2
  * Adequate organ function
  *…

related_trials.brief_title: Re-irradiation Therapy of Locally Recurrent Rectal Cancer With Carbon Ion
related_trials.brief_summary: Prospective phase II clinical study aim to explore the clinical outcome of patients with unresectable locally recurrent rectal cancer (LRRC) treated with re-irradiation by carbon ion radiotherapy (CIRT)

interventions.name: Carbon ion radiation therapy DT: 74Gy/20 fractions

related_trials.brief_title: Radiotherapy & Total Neoadjuvant Therapy for Recurrent Rectal Cancer in Previously Irradiated Patients, an Italian Association for Radiotherapy and Clinical Oncology (AIRO)-GI Platform: a Multi-centre Prospective Observational Study
related_trials.brief_summary: The introduction of neoadjuvant chemoradiation therapy (CRT) and the use of advanced surgical techniques have led to a reduction in mortality and recurrence rates for rectal cancer, the rate of which currently stands at 4-8%. Complete cytoreduction (achieving R0) of local recurrence is the main factor correlating with survival, but surgery can often be very complex because of the change in anatomical planes caused by previous surgery. Reirradiation of the recurrence may increase the rate of…
related_trials.relevance_phrase: reirradiation + TNT in previously irradiated LRRC

related_trials.brief_title: Hypofractionated Radiotherapy Plus Immunotherapy Versus Conventional Radiotherapy in Locally Recurrent Rectal Cancer
related_trials.brief_summary: TORCH-R2 is a prospective, multicenter, randomized, phase II clinical trial. Patients aged 18 years or older with pelvic recurrence rectal cancer without synchronous distant metastases or recent chemo- and/or radiotherapy treatment, Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group performance status of 0-1will be enrolled . papers.title: Executive summary of American Radium Society Appropriate Use Criteria for the treatment of locoregionally recurrent rectal cancer
papers.abstract: Abstract This literature‐based systematic review and associated guidelines provide evidence‐based paradigms for the management of locoregionally recurrent rectal cancer (LRRC). This multispecialty committee included gastrointestinal radiation and medical oncology, gastroenterology, radiology, and colorectal surgery. As is the standard, the previously described American Radium Society Appropriate Use Criteria methodology for this project was followed rigorously, with the Population, Intervention, Comparator, Outcome, Timing, and Study Design framework and Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta‐Analyses methodology to assess the evidence. RAND/University of California Los Angeles consensus methodology (modified Delphi) was used to rate the appropriateness of treatment options. Published between January 1, 2013, and July 16, 2025, 116 peer‐reviewed trials provided the evidence: 10 were well‐designed randomized phase 2/3 trials, 29 were moderately well designed trials that accounted for most common biases (matched cohort and phase 2), 76 trials had design limitations (retrospective), and one was a meta‐analysis. Clinical cases were created as examples to illustrate current acceptable management of LRRC. Treatment and prognosis are influenced by prior therapy and the site(s) and extent of LRRC. The ability to achieve a margin‐negative surgical resection is the ultimate determinant of survival and local control. Preoperative systemic therapy, radiation therapy, or a combination of the two can facilitate tumor downsizing and improve the likelihood of a margin‐negative resection. An individualized multidisciplinary approach is required to ensure the best outcome. Although this review does not suggest a major alteration of current practice, it provides reassuring evidence of the importance of combined‐modality therapy.
